revision.text:
{{Infobox mythological creature | name = Zenki and Goki | image = En_no_Gyōja_sculpture_at_Kura-dera.jpg | image_size = 250px | caption = Statue of [[En no Gyōja]] flanked by Zenki (left) and Goki (right). | grouping = [[Oni (folklore)|Oni]] | mythological_origins = [[Japanese mythology]] | region = [[Japan]] | similar_creatures = [[Shikigami]] }}
'''Zenki''' {{nihongo|'''Zenki'''|前鬼||lit. "Front Demon"}} and {{nihongo|'''Goki'''|後鬼||lit. "Back Demon"}} are a husband and wife pair of [[Oni (folklore)|oni]] (demons or ogres) in [[Japanese folklore]] who serve as the familiars and protectors of the [[Shugendō]] founder [[En no Gyōja]].
They are frequently depicted in Japanese Buddhist art flanking En no Gyōja, often holding tools to assist him in his mountain asceticism. Zenki is traditionally depicted as male and red, while Goki is depicted as female and blue.<ref name="Miyake"/>
== Legend == According to tradition, Zenki and Goki were originally a pair of malevolent demons who lived in the Ikoma Mountains (or, in some variations, the [[Omine|Ōmine]] mountain range) near [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]]. They terrorized local villagers and stole their children.
[[En no Gyōja]], utilizing his spiritual powers, hid one of the demons' children (traditionally the youngest of five) in an iron cauldron to teach them the pain of losing a child. Overcome with grief, the demon couple begged for the child's return. En no Gyōja agreed on the condition that they reform their ways, cease killing, and follow the path of Buddha.<ref name="Blacker">{{cite book |last=Blacker |first=Carmen |title=The Catalpa Bow: Essays on Shamanistic Practices in Japan |publisher=Routledge |year=1999 |isbn=978-1873410851 |page=242}}</ref>
Following their conversion, they became En no Gyōja's devoted servants. Zenki, the husband, took the name '''Gikaku''' (義覚) or '''Giga''' (義学), and Goki, the wife, took the name '''Gigen''' (義賢) or '''Giken''' (義玄).<ref name="NaraMuseum">{{cite web |title=Statues of En no Gyoja, Zenki, and Goki |url=https://www.narahaku.go.jp/english/collection/1183-0.html |publisher=Nara National Museum |access-date=2023-10-27}}</ref>
=== Names and Symbolism === Their names reflect their physical positioning in relation to their master during mountain treks: * '''Zenki''' ("Front Demon") walks ahead of En no Gyōja, clearing the path with an iron axe. He represents [[Yang]] (or ''Yō'' in Japanese), action, and protection. * '''Goki''' ("Back Demon") walks behind, carrying a water vessel (often containing Holy Water or ''aka'') and supplies. She represents [[Yin]] (or ''In'' in Japanese), receptivity, and sustenance.
Together, they represent the harmonious balance of dualities necessary for spiritual enlightenment within the Shugendō tradition.<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last=Earhart |first=H. Byron |title=A Religious Study of the Mount Haguro Sect of Shugendo |publisher=Sophia University |year=1970 |pages=25–30}}</ref>
== Iconography == In religious statuary and paintings (such as [[mandala]]s), the pair are almost always shown flanking En no Gyōja.
* ''Zenki'': Usually depicted with red skin, bulging eyes, and two horns. He wears a deer skin and carries a large iron [[axe]] meant to clear dense vegetation. * ''Goki'': Usually depicted with blue or green skin. In some depictions, she has no horns, appearing more human-like than her husband. She carries a water pitcher (''suibyo'') and sometimes a basket of fruits or firewood.
== Descendants == Unlike most ''oni'' in Japanese folklore, Zenki and Goki are traditionally believed to have established a human lineage. According to lore, after En no Gyōja passed away or ascended, the couple settled in the village of Shimokitayama in the Yoshino district of Nara Prefecture.
They are said to have had five children, who became the ancestors of the "Gokigen" (Five Demon relation) families. These families historically served as the protectors of the practice sites at [[Mount Ōmine]].<ref>{{cite book |last=Bouchy |first=Anne-Marie |title=Shugendô: The Way of the Mountain Monks |publisher=Sasshina |year=2000 |isbn=978-2954653526}}</ref>
Historically, residents of the Zenki settlement (Zenki-guchi) in Shimokitayama claim direct descent from the pair. This lineage was acknowledged during the [[Edo period]], granting the families specific privileges and tax exemptions from the Shogunate in exchange for their maintenance of the pilgrim lodges (''shukubo'').<ref name="Miyake">{{cite book |last=Miyake |first=Hitoshi |title=Shugendo: Essays on the Structure of Japanese Folk Religion |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, University of Michigan |year=2001 |isbn=978-0939512784 |pages=67–69}}</ref>
Because of this ancestry, these families traditionally do not use the phrase "Oni wa soto" (Demons out!) during the [[Setsubun]] bean-throwing festival; instead, they may say "Oni wa uchi" (Demons in) or simply remain silent, regarding the demons as honorable ancestors rather than monsters to be expelled.
== Popular culture == * ''Anime/Manga'': The characters appear in various supernatural manga, including ''[[Zenki]]'' (Kishin Dōji Zenki), where Zenki is the titular protagonist bound to a descendant of En no Gyōja. * ''Video Games'': They appear as demons or "Personas" in the ''[[Megami Tensei]]'' franchise. * ''Literature'': They are featured in the fantasy novel ''[[Teito Monogatari]]''.
== See also == * [[Shugendō]] * [[En no Gyōja]] * [[Oni (folklore)]] * [[Kishimojin]] (A Buddhist deity with a similar origin story involving a hidden child)
== References == {{Reflist}}
''Note: Generated using Google Gemini 3 Pro''.
== External links == * [https://www.narahaku.go.jp/english/collection/1183-0.html Nara National Museum - Statues of En no Gyoja, Zenki and Goki]
{{DEFAULTSORT:Zenki and Goki}} [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Shugendō]] [[Category:Yōkai]] [[Category:Mythological pairs]] [[Category:Fictional married couples]]

{{Infobox mythological creature | name = Zenki and Goki | image = En_no_Gyōja_sculpture_at_Kura-dera.jpg | image_size = 250px | caption = Statue of [[En no Gyōja]] flanked by Zenki (left) and Goki (right). | grouping = [[Oni (folklore)|Oni]] | mythological_origins = [[Japanese mythology]] | region = [[Japan]] | similar_creatures = [[Shikigami]] }}
'''Zenki''' {{nihongo|'''Zenki'''|前鬼||lit. "Front Demon"}} and {{nihongo|'''Goki'''|後鬼||lit. "Back Demon"}} are a husband and wife pair of [[Oni (folklore)|oni]] (demons or ogres) in [[Japanese folklore]] who serve as the familiars and protectors of the [[Shugendō]] founder [[En no Gyōja]].
They are frequently depicted in Japanese Buddhist art flanking En no Gyōja, often holding tools to assist him in his mountain asceticism. Zenki is traditionally depicted as male and red, while Goki is depicted as female and blue.<ref name="Miyake"/>
== Legend == According to tradition, Zenki and Goki were originally a pair of malevolent demons who lived in the Ikoma Mountains (or, in some variations, the [[Omine|Ōmine]] mountain range) near [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]]. They terrorized local villagers and stole their children.
[[En no Gyōja]], utilizing his spiritual powers, hid one of the demons' children (traditionally the youngest of five) in an iron cauldron to teach them the pain of losing a child. Overcome with grief, the demon couple begged for the child's return. En no Gyōja agreed on the condition that they reform their ways, cease killing, and follow the path of Buddha.<ref name="Blacker">{{cite book |last=Blacker |first=Carmen |title=The Catalpa Bow: Essays on Shamanistic Practices in Japan |publisher=Routledge |year=1999 |isbn=978-1873410851 |page=242}}</ref>
Following their conversion, they became En no Gyōja's devoted servants. Zenki, the husband, took the name '''Gikaku''' (義覚) or '''Giga''' (義学), and Goki, the wife, took the name '''Gigen''' (義賢) or '''Giken''' (義玄).<ref name="NaraMuseum">{{cite web |title=Statues of En no Gyoja, Zenki, and Goki |url=https://www.narahaku.go.jp/english/collection/1183-0.html |publisher=Nara National Museum |access-date=2023-10-27}}</ref>
=== Names and Symbolism === Their names reflect their physical positioning in relation to their master during mountain treks: * '''Zenki''' ("Front Demon") walks ahead of En no Gyōja, clearing the path with an iron axe. He represents [[Yang]] (or ''Yō'' in Japanese), action, and protection. * '''Goki''' ("Back Demon") walks behind, carrying a water vessel (often containing Holy Water or ''aka'') and supplies. She represents [[Yin]] (or ''In'' in Japanese), receptivity, and sustenance.
Together, they represent the harmonious balance of dualities necessary for spiritual enlightenment within the Shugendō tradition.<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last=Earhart |first=H. Byron |title=A Religious Study of the Mount Haguro Sect of Shugendo |publisher=Sophia University |year=1970 |pages=25–30}}</ref>
== Iconography == In religious statuary and paintings (such as [[mandala]]s), the pair are almost always shown flanking En no Gyōja.
* ''Zenki'': Usually depicted with red skin, bulging eyes, and two horns. He wears a deer skin and carries a large iron [[axe]] meant to clear dense vegetation. * ''Goki'': Usually depicted with blue or green skin. In some depictions, she has no horns, appearing more human-like than her husband. She carries a water pitcher (''suibyo'') and sometimes a basket of fruits or firewood.
== Descendants == Unlike most ''oni'' in Japanese folklore, Zenki and Goki are traditionally believed to have established a human lineage. According to lore, after En no Gyōja passed away or ascended, the couple settled in the village of Shimokitayama in the Yoshino district of Nara Prefecture.
They are said to have had five children, who became the ancestors of the "Gokigen" (Five Demon relation) families. These families historically served as the protectors of the practice sites at [[Mount Ōmine]].<ref>{{cite book |last=Bouchy |first=Anne-Marie |title=Shugendô: The Way of the Mountain Monks |publisher=Sasshina |year=2000 |isbn=978-2954653526}}</ref>
Historically, residents of the Zenki settlement (Zenki-guchi) in Shimokitayama claim direct descent from the pair. This lineage was acknowledged during the [[Edo period]], granting the families specific privileges and tax exemptions from the Shogunate in exchange for their maintenance of the pilgrim lodges (''shukubo'').<ref name="Miyake">{{cite book |last=Miyake |first=Hitoshi |title=Shugendo: Essays on the Structure of Japanese Folk Religion |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, University of Michigan |year=2001 |isbn=978-0939512784 |pages=67–69}}</ref>
Because of this ancestry, these families traditionally do not use the phrase "Oni wa soto" (Demons out!) during the [[Setsubun]] bean-throwing festival; instead, they may say "Oni wa uchi" (Demons in) or simply remain silent, regarding the demons as honorable ancestors rather than monsters to be expelled.
== Popular culture == * ''Anime/Manga'': The characters appear in various supernatural manga, including ''[[Zenki]]'' (Kishin Dōji Zenki), where Zenki is the titular protagonist bound to a descendant of En no Gyōja. * ''Video Games'': They appear as demons or "Personas" in the ''[[Megami Tensei]]'' franchise. * ''Literature'': They are featured in the fantasy novel ''[[Teito Monogatari]]''.
== See also == * [[Shugendō]] * [[En no Gyōja]] * [[Oni (folklore)]] * [[Kishimojin]] (A Buddhist deity with a similar origin story involving a hidden child)

{{Infobox mythological creature | name = Zenki and Goki | image = En_no_Gyōja_sculpture_at_Kura-dera.jpg | image_size = 250px | caption = Statue of [[En no Gyōja]] flanked by Zenki (left) and Goki (right). | grouping = [[Oni (folklore)|Oni]] | mythological_origins = [[Japanese mythology]] | region = [[Japan]] | similar_creatures = [[Shikigami]] }}
'''Zenki''' {{nihongo|'''Zenki'''|前鬼||lit. "Front Demon"}} and {{nihongo|'''Goki'''|後鬼||lit. "Back Demon"}} are a husband and wife pair of [[Oni (folklore)|oni]] (demons or ogres) in [[Japanese folklore]] who serve as the familiars and protectors of the [[Shugendō]] founder [[En no Gyōja]].
They are frequently depicted in Japanese Buddhist art flanking En no Gyōja, often holding tools to assist him in his mountain asceticism. Zenki is traditionally depicted as male and red, while Goki is depicted as female and blue.<ref name="Miyake"/>
== Legend == According to tradition, Zenki and Goki were originally a pair of malevolent demons who lived in the Ikoma Mountains (or, in some variations, the [[Omine|Ōmine]] mountain range) near [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]]. They terrorized local villagers and stole their children.
[[En no Gyōja]], utilizing his spiritual powers, hid one of the demons' children (traditionally the youngest of five) in an iron cauldron to teach them the pain of losing a child. Overcome with grief, the demon couple begged for the child's return. En no Gyōja agreed on the condition that they reform their ways, cease killing, and follow the path of Buddha.<ref name="Blacker">{{cite book |last=Blacker |first=Carmen |title=The Catalpa Bow: Essays on Shamanistic Practices in Japan |publisher=Routledge |year=1999 |isbn=978-1873410851 |page=242}}</ref>
Following their conversion, they became En no Gyōja's devoted servants. Zenki, the husband, took the name '''Gikaku''' (義覚) or '''Giga''' (義学), and Goki, the wife, took the name '''Gigen''' (義賢) or '''Giken''' (義玄).<ref name="NaraMuseum">{{cite web |title=Statues of En no Gyoja, Zenki, and Goki |url=https://www.narahaku.go.jp/english/collection/1183-0.html |publisher=Nara National Museum |access-date=2023-10-27}}</ref>
=== Names and Symbolism === Their names reflect their physical positioning in relation to their master during mountain treks: * '''Zenki''' ("Front Demon") walks ahead of En no Gyōja, clearing the path with an iron axe. He represents [[Yang]] (or ''Yō'' in Japanese), action, and protection. * '''Goki''' ("Back Demon") walks behind, carrying a water vessel (often containing Holy Water or ''aka'') and supplies. She represents [[Yin]] (or ''In'' in Japanese), receptivity, and sustenance.
Together, they represent the harmonious balance of dualities necessary for spiritual enlightenment within the Shugendō tradition.<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last=Earhart |first=H. Byron |title=A Religious Study of the Mount Haguro Sect of Shugendo |publisher=Sophia University |year=1970 |pages=25–30}}</ref>
== Iconography == In religious statuary and paintings (such as [[mandala]]s), the pair are almost always shown flanking En no Gyōja.
* ''Zenki'': Usually depicted with red skin, bulging eyes, and two horns. He wears a deer skin and carries a large iron [[axe]] meant to clear dense vegetation. * ''Goki'': Usually depicted with blue or green skin. In some depictions, she has no horns, appearing more human-like than her husband. She carries a water pitcher (''suibyo'') and sometimes a basket of fruits or firewood.
== Descendants == Unlike most ''oni'' in Japanese folklore, Zenki and Goki are traditionally believed to have established a human lineage. According to lore, after En no Gyōja passed away or ascended, the couple settled in the village of Shimokitayama in the Yoshino district of Nara Prefecture.
They are said to have had five children, who became the ancestors of the "Gokigen" (Five Demon relation) families. These families historically served as the protectors of the practice sites at [[Mount Ōmine]].<ref>{{cite book |last=Bouchy |first=Anne-Marie |title=Shugendô: The Way of the Mountain Monks |publisher=Sasshina |year=2000 |isbn=978-2954653526}}</ref>
Historically, residents of the Zenki settlement (Zenki-guchi) in Shimokitayama claim direct descent from the pair. This lineage was acknowledged during the [[Edo period]], granting the families specific privileges and tax exemptions from the Shogunate in exchange for their maintenance of the pilgrim lodges (''shukubo'').<ref name="Miyake">{{cite book |last=Miyake |first=Hitoshi |title=Shugendo: Essays on the Structure of Japanese Folk Religion |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, University of Michigan |year=2001 |isbn=978-0939512784 |pages=67–69}}</ref>
Because of this ancestry, these families traditionally do not use the phrase "Oni wa soto" (Demons out!) during the [[Setsubun]] bean-throwing festival; instead, they may say "Oni wa uchi" (Demons in) or simply remain silent, regarding the demons as honorable ancestors rather than monsters to be expelled.
== Popular culture == * ''Anime/Manga'': The characters appear in various supernatural manga, including ''[[Zenki]]'' (Kishin Dōji Zenki), where Zenki is the titular protagonist bound to a descendant of En no Gyōja. * ''Video Games'': They appear as demons or "Personas" in the ''[[Megami Tensei]]'' franchise. * ''Literature'': They are featured in the fantasy novel ''[[Teito Monogatari]]''.
== See also == * [[Shugendō]] * [[En no Gyōja]] * [[Oni (folklore)]] * [[Kishimojin]] (A Buddhist deity with a similar origin story involving a hidden child)

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Zenki and Goki]][[ja:前鬼・後鬼]][[zh:前鬼與後鬼]] {{Infobox mythological creature | name = Zenki and Goki | image = En_no_Gyōja_sculpture_at_Kura-dera.jpg | image_size = 250px | caption = Statue of [[En no Gyōja]] flanked by Zenki (left) and Goki (right). | grouping = [[Oni (folklore)|Oni]] | mythological_origins = [[Japanese mythology]] | region = [[Japan]] | similar_creatures = [[Shikigami]] }}
'''Zenki''' {{nihongo|'''Zenki'''|前鬼||lit. "Front Demon"}} and {{nihongo|'''Goki'''|後鬼||lit. "Back Demon"}} are a husband and wife pair of [[Oni (folklore)|oni]] (demons or ogres) in [[Japanese folklore]] who serve as the familiars and protectors of the [[Shugendō]] founder [[En no Gyōja]].
They are frequently depicted in Japanese Buddhist art flanking En no Gyōja, often holding tools to assist him in his mountain asceticism. Zenki is traditionally depicted as male and red, while Goki is depicted as female and blue.<ref name="Miyake"/>
== Legend == According to tradition, Zenki and Goki were originally a pair of malevolent demons who lived in the Ikoma Mountains (or, in some variations, the [[Omine|Ōmine]] mountain range) near [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]]. They terrorized local villagers and stole their children.
[[En no Gyōja]], utilizing his spiritual powers, hid one of the demons' children (traditionally the youngest of five) in an iron cauldron to teach them the pain of losing a child. Overcome with grief, the demon couple begged for the child's return. En no Gyōja agreed on the condition that they reform their ways, cease killing, and follow the path of Buddha.<ref name="Blacker">{{cite book |last=Blacker |first=Carmen |title=The Catalpa Bow: Essays on Shamanistic Practices in Japan |publisher=Routledge |year=1999 |isbn=978-1873410851 |page=242}}</ref>
Following their conversion, they became En no Gyōja's devoted servants. Zenki, the husband, took the name '''Gikaku''' (義覚) or '''Giga''' (義学), and Goki, the wife, took the name '''Gigen''' (義賢) or '''Giken''' (義玄).<ref name="NaraMuseum">{{cite web |title=Statues of En no Gyoja, Zenki, and Goki |url=https://www.narahaku.go.jp/english/collection/1183-0.html |publisher=Nara National Museum |access-date=2023-10-27}}</ref>
=== Names and Symbolism === Their names reflect their physical positioning in relation to their master during mountain treks: * '''Zenki''' ("Front Demon") walks ahead of En no Gyōja, clearing the path with an iron axe. He represents [[Yang]] (or ''Yō'' in Japanese), action, and protection. * '''Goki''' ("Back Demon") walks behind, carrying a water vessel (often containing Holy Water or ''aka'') and supplies. She represents [[Yin]] (or ''In'' in Japanese), receptivity, and sustenance.
Together, they represent the harmonious balance of dualities necessary for spiritual enlightenment within the Shugendō tradition.<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last=Earhart |first=H. Byron |title=A Religious Study of the Mount Haguro Sect of Shugendo |publisher=Sophia University |year=1970 |pages=25–30}}</ref>
== Iconography == In religious statuary and paintings (such as [[mandala]]s), the pair are almost always shown flanking En no Gyōja.
* ''Zenki'': Usually depicted with red skin, bulging eyes, and two horns. He wears a deer skin and carries a large iron [[axe]] meant to clear dense vegetation. * ''Goki'': Usually depicted with blue or green skin. In some depictions, she has no horns, appearing more human-like than her husband. She carries a water pitcher (''suibyo'') and sometimes a basket of fruits or firewood.
== Descendants == Unlike most ''oni'' in Japanese folklore, Zenki and Goki are traditionally believed to have established a human lineage. According to lore, after En no Gyōja passed away or ascended, the couple settled in the village of Shimokitayama in the Yoshino district of Nara Prefecture.
They are said to have had five children, who became the ancestors of the "Gokigen" (Five Demon relation) families. These families historically served as the protectors of the practice sites at [[Mount Ōmine]].<ref>{{cite book |last=Bouchy |first=Anne-Marie |title=Shugendô: The Way of the Mountain Monks |publisher=Sasshina |year=2000 |isbn=978-2954653526}}</ref>
Historically, residents of the Zenki settlement (Zenki-guchi) in Shimokitayama claim direct descent from the pair. This lineage was acknowledged during the [[Edo period]], granting the families specific privileges and tax exemptions from the Shogunate in exchange for their maintenance of the pilgrim lodges (''shukubo'').<ref name="Miyake">{{cite book |last=Miyake |first=Hitoshi |title=Shugendo: Essays on the Structure of Japanese Folk Religion |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, University of Michigan |year=2001 |isbn=978-0939512784 |pages=67–69}}</ref>
Because of this ancestry, these families traditionally do not use the phrase "Oni wa soto" (Demons out!) during the [[Setsubun]] bean-throwing festival; instead, they may say "Oni wa uchi" (Demons in) or simply remain silent, regarding the demons as honorable ancestors rather than monsters to be expelled.
== Popular culture == * ''Anime/Manga'': The characters appear in various supernatural manga, including ''[[Zenki]]'' (Kishin Dōji Zenki), where Zenki is the titular protagonist bound to a descendant of En no Gyōja. * ''Video Games'': They appear as demons or "Personas" in the ''[[Megami Tensei]]'' franchise. * ''Literature'': They are featured in the fantasy novel ''[[Teito Monogatari]]''.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Zenki and Goki]][[ja:前鬼・後鬼]][[zh:前鬼與後鬼]] {{Infobox mythological creature | name = Zenki and Goki | image = En_no_Gyōja_sculpture_at_Kura-dera.jpg | image_size = 250px | caption = Statue of [[En no Gyōja]] flanked by Zenki (left) and Goki (right). | grouping = [[Oni (folklore)|Oni]] | mythological_origins = [[Japanese mythology]] | region = [[Japan]] | similar_creatures = [[Shikigami]] }}
'''Zenki''' {{nihongo|'''Zenki'''|前鬼||lit. "Front Demon"}} and {{nihongo|'''Goki'''|後鬼||lit. "Back Demon"}} are a husband and wife pair of [[Oni (folklore)|oni]] (demons or ogres) in [[Japanese folklore]] who serve as the familiars and protectors of the [[Shugendō]] founder [[En no Gyōja]].
They are frequently depicted in Japanese Buddhist art flanking En no Gyōja, often holding tools to assist him in his mountain asceticism. Zenki is traditionally depicted as male and red, while Goki is depicted as female and blue.<ref name="Miyake"/>
== Legend == According to tradition, Zenki and Goki were originally a pair of malevolent demons who lived in the Ikoma Mountains (or, in some variations, the [[Omine|Ōmine]] mountain range) near [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]]. They terrorized local villagers and stole their children.
[[En no Gyōja]], utilizing his spiritual powers, hid one of the demons' children (traditionally the youngest of five) in an iron cauldron to teach them the pain of losing a child. Overcome with grief, the demon couple begged for the child's return. En no Gyōja agreed on the condition that they reform their ways, cease killing, and follow the path of Buddha.<ref name="Blacker">{{cite book |last=Blacker |first=Carmen |title=The Catalpa Bow: Essays on Shamanistic Practices in Japan |publisher=Routledge |year=1999 |isbn=978-1873410851 |page=242}}</ref>
Following their conversion, they became En no Gyōja's devoted servants. Zenki, the husband, took the name '''Gikaku''' (義覚) or '''Giga''' (義学), and Goki, the wife, took the name '''Gigen''' (義賢) or '''Giken''' (義玄).<ref name="NaraMuseum">{{cite web |title=Statues of En no Gyoja, Zenki, and Goki |url=https://www.narahaku.go.jp/english/collection/1183-0.html |publisher=Nara National Museum |access-date=2023-10-27}}</ref>
=== Names and Symbolism === Their names reflect their physical positioning in relation to their master during mountain treks: * '''Zenki''' ("Front Demon") walks ahead of En no Gyōja, clearing the path with an iron axe. He represents [[Yang]] (or ''Yō'' in Japanese), action, and protection. * '''Goki''' ("Back Demon") walks behind, carrying a water vessel (often containing Holy Water or ''aka'') and supplies. She represents [[Yin]] (or ''In'' in Japanese), receptivity, and sustenance.
Together, they represent the harmonious balance of dualities necessary for spiritual enlightenment within the Shugendō tradition.<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last=Earhart |first=H. Byron |title=A Religious Study of the Mount Haguro Sect of Shugendo |publisher=Sophia University |year=1970 |pages=25–30}}</ref>
== Iconography == In religious statuary and paintings (such as [[mandala]]s), the pair are almost always shown flanking En no Gyōja.
* ''Zenki'': Usually depicted with red skin, bulging eyes, and two horns. He wears a deer skin and carries a large iron [[axe]] meant to clear dense vegetation. * ''Goki'': Usually depicted with blue or green skin. In some depictions, she has no horns, appearing more human-like than her husband. She carries a water pitcher (''suibyo'') and sometimes a basket of fruits or firewood.
== Descendants == Unlike most ''oni'' in Japanese folklore, Zenki and Goki are traditionally believed to have established a human lineage. According to lore, after En no Gyōja passed away or ascended, the couple settled in the village of Shimokitayama in the Yoshino district of Nara Prefecture.
They are said to have had five children, who became the ancestors of the "Gokigen" (Five Demon relation) families. These families historically served as the protectors of the practice sites at [[Mount Ōmine]].<ref>{{cite book |last=Bouchy |first=Anne-Marie |title=Shugendô: The Way of the Mountain Monks |publisher=Sasshina |year=2000 |isbn=978-2954653526}}</ref>
Historically, residents of the Zenki settlement (Zenki-guchi) in Shimokitayama claim direct descent from the pair. This lineage was acknowledged during the [[Edo period]], granting the families specific privileges and tax exemptions from the Shogunate in exchange for their maintenance of the pilgrim lodges (''shukubo'').<ref name="Miyake">{{cite book |last=Miyake |first=Hitoshi |title=Shugendo: Essays on the Structure of Japanese Folk Religion |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, University of Michigan |year=2001 |isbn=978-0939512784 |pages=67–69}}</ref>
Because of this ancestry, these families traditionally do not use the phrase "Oni wa soto" (Demons out!) during the [[Setsubun]] bean-throwing festival; instead, they may say "Oni wa uchi" (Demons in) or simply remain silent, regarding the demons as honorable ancestors rather than monsters to be expelled.
== Popular culture == * ''Anime/Manga'': The characters appear in various supernatural manga, including ''[[Zenki]]'' (Kishin Dōji Zenki), where Zenki is the titular protagonist bound to a descendant of En no Gyōja. * ''Video Games'': They appear as demons or "Personas" in the ''[[Megami Tensei]]'' franchise. * ''Literature'': They are featured in the fantasy novel ''[[Teito Monogatari]]''.
== See also == * [[Shugendō]] * [[En no Gyōja]] * [[Oni (folklore)]] * [[Kishimojin]] (A Buddhist deity with a similar origin story involving a hidden child)
== References == {{Reflist}}
''Note: Generated using Google Gemini 3 Pro''.
== External links == * [https://www.narahaku.go.jp/english/collection/1183-0.html Nara National Museum - Statues of En no Gyoja, Zenki and Goki]
{{DEFAULTSORT:Zenki and Goki}} [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Shugendō]] [[Category:Yōkai]] [[Category:Mythological pairs]] [[Category:Fictional married couples]] {{wikidata link|Q11397864}} [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

{{Infobox mythological creature | name = Zenki and Goki | image = En_no_Gyōja_sculpture_at_Kura-dera.jpg | image_size = 250px | caption = Statue of [[En no Gyōja]] flanked by Zenki (left) and Goki (right). | grouping = [[Oni (folklore)|Oni]] | mythological_origins = [[Japanese mythology]] | region = [[Japan]] | similar_creatures = [[Shikigami]] }}
'''Zenki''' {{nihongo|'''Zenki'''|前鬼||lit. "Front Demon"}} and {{nihongo|'''Goki'''|後鬼||lit. "Back Demon"}} are a husband and wife pair of [[Oni (folklore)|oni]] (demons or ogres) in [[Japanese folklore]] who serve as the familiars and protectors of the [[Shugendō]] founder [[En no Gyōja]].
They are frequently depicted in Japanese Buddhist art flanking En no Gyōja, often holding tools to assist him in his mountain asceticism. Zenki is traditionally depicted as male and red, while Goki is depicted as female and blue.<ref name="Miyake"/>
== Legend == According to tradition, Zenki and Goki were originally a pair of malevolent demons who lived in the Ikoma Mountains (or, in some variations, the [[Omine|Ōmine]] mountain range) near [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]]. They terrorized local villagers and stole their children.
[[En no Gyōja]], utilizing his spiritual powers, hid one of the demons' children (traditionally the youngest of five) in an iron cauldron to teach them the pain of losing a child. Overcome with grief, the demon couple begged for the child's return. En no Gyōja agreed on the condition that they reform their ways, cease killing, and follow the path of Buddha.<ref name="Blacker">{{cite book |last=Blacker |first=Carmen |title=The Catalpa Bow: Essays on Shamanistic Practices in Japan |publisher=Routledge |year=1999 |isbn=978-1873410851 |page=242}}</ref>
Following their conversion, they became En no Gyōja's devoted servants. Zenki, the husband, took the name '''Gikaku''' (義覚) or '''Giga''' (義学), and Goki, the wife, took the name '''Gigen''' (義賢) or '''Giken''' (義玄).<ref name="NaraMuseum">{{cite web |title=Statues of En no Gyoja, Zenki, and Goki |url=https://www.narahaku.go.jp/english/collection/1183-0.html |publisher=Nara National Museum |access-date=2023-10-27}}</ref>
=== Names and Symbolism === Their names reflect their physical positioning in relation to their master during mountain treks: * '''Zenki''' ("Front Demon") walks ahead of En no Gyōja, clearing the path with an iron axe. He represents [[Yang]] (or ''Yō'' in Japanese), action, and protection. * '''Goki''' ("Back Demon") walks behind, carrying a water vessel (often containing Holy Water or ''aka'') and supplies. She represents [[Yin]] (or ''In'' in Japanese), receptivity, and sustenance.
Together, they represent the harmonious balance of dualities necessary for spiritual enlightenment within the Shugendō tradition.<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last=Earhart |first=H. Byron |title=A Religious Study of the Mount Haguro Sect of Shugendo |publisher=Sophia University |year=1970 |pages=25–30}}</ref>
== Iconography == In religious statuary and paintings (such as [[mandala]]s), the pair are almost always shown flanking En no Gyōja.
* ''Zenki'': Usually depicted with red skin, bulging eyes, and two horns. He wears a deer skin and carries a large iron [[axe]] meant to clear dense vegetation. * ''Goki'': Usually depicted with blue or green skin. In some depictions, she has no horns, appearing more human-like than her husband. She carries a water pitcher (''suibyo'') and sometimes a basket of fruits or firewood.
== Descendants == Unlike most ''oni'' in Japanese folklore, Zenki and Goki are traditionally believed to have established a human lineage. According to lore, after En no Gyōja passed away or ascended, the couple settled in the village of Shimokitayama in the Yoshino district of Nara Prefecture.
They are said to have had five children, who became the ancestors of the "Gokigen" (Five Demon relation) families. These families historically served as the protectors of the practice sites at [[Mount Ōmine]].<ref>{{cite book |last=Bouchy |first=Anne-Marie |title=Shugendô: The Way of the Mountain Monks |publisher=Sasshina |year=2000 |isbn=978-2954653526}}</ref>
Historically, residents of the Zenki settlement (Zenki-guchi) in Shimokitayama claim direct descent from the pair. This lineage was acknowledged during the [[Edo period]], granting the families specific privileges and tax exemptions from the Shogunate in exchange for their maintenance of the pilgrim lodges (''shukubo'').<ref name="Miyake">{{cite book |last=Miyake |first=Hitoshi |title=Shugendo: Essays on the Structure of Japanese Folk Religion |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, University of Michigan |year=2001 |isbn=978-0939512784 |pages=67–69}}</ref>
Because of this ancestry, these families traditionally do not use the phrase "Oni wa soto" (Demons out!) during the [[Setsubun]] bean-throwing festival; instead, they may say "Oni wa uchi" (Demons in) or simply remain silent, regarding the demons as honorable ancestors rather than monsters to be expelled.
== Popular culture == * ''Anime/Manga'': The characters appear in various supernatural manga, including ''[[Zenki]]'' (Kishin Dōji Zenki), where Zenki is the titular protagonist bound to a descendant of En no Gyōja. * ''Video Games'': They appear as demons or "Personas" in the ''[[Megami Tensei]]'' franchise. * ''Literature'': They are featured in the fantasy novel ''[[Teito Monogatari]]''.
== See also == * [[Shugendō]] * [[En no Gyōja]] * [[Oni (folklore)]] * [[Kishimojin]] (A Buddhist deity with a similar origin story involving a hidden child)
== References == {{Reflist}}
''Note: Generated using Google Gemini 3 Pro''.
== External links == * [https://www.narahaku.go.jp/english/collection/1183-0.html Nara National Museum - Statues of En no Gyoja, Zenki and Goki]
{{DEFAULTSORT:Zenki and Goki}} [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Shugendō]] [[Category:Yōkai]] [[Category:Mythological pairs]] [[Category:Fictional married couples]]

revision.comment: adding dummy category to make categories work
revision.text:
{{Infobox mythological creature | name = Zenki and Goki | image = En_no_Gyōja_sculpture_at_Kura-dera.jpg | image_size = 250px | caption = Statue of [[En no Gyōja]] flanked by Zenki (left) and Goki (right). | grouping = [[Oni (folklore)|Oni]] | mythological_origins = [[Japanese mythology]] | region = [[Japan]] | similar_creatures = [[Shikigami]] }}
'''Zenki''' {{nihongo|'''Zenki'''|前鬼||lit. "Front Demon"}} and {{nihongo|'''Goki'''|後鬼||lit. "Back Demon"}} are a husband and wife pair of [[Oni (folklore)|oni]] (demons or ogres) in [[Japanese folklore]] who serve as the familiars and protectors of the [[Shugendō]] founder [[En no Gyōja]].
They are frequently depicted in Japanese Buddhist art flanking En no Gyōja, often holding tools to assist him in his mountain asceticism. Zenki is traditionally depicted as male and red, while Goki is depicted as female and blue.<ref name="Miyake"/>
== Legend == According to tradition, Zenki and Goki were originally a pair of malevolent demons who lived in the Ikoma Mountains (or, in some variations, the [[Omine|Ōmine]] mountain range) near [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]]. They terrorized local villagers and stole their children.
[[En no Gyōja]], utilizing his spiritual powers, hid one of the demons' children (traditionally the youngest of five) in an iron cauldron to teach them the pain of losing a child. Overcome with grief, the demon couple begged for the child's return. En no Gyōja agreed on the condition that they reform their ways, cease killing, and follow the path of Buddha.<ref name="Blacker">{{cite book |last=Blacker |first=Carmen |title=The Catalpa Bow: Essays on Shamanistic Practices in Japan |publisher=Routledge |year=1999 |isbn=978-1873410851 |page=242}}</ref>
Following their conversion, they became En no Gyōja's devoted servants. Zenki, the husband, took the name '''Gikaku''' (義覚) or '''Giga''' (義学), and Goki, the wife, took the name '''Gigen''' (義賢) or '''Giken''' (義玄).<ref name="NaraMuseum">{{cite web |title=Statues of En no Gyoja, Zenki, and Goki |url=https://www.narahaku.go.jp/english/collection/1183-0.html |publisher=Nara National Museum |access-date=2023-10-27}}</ref>
=== Names and Symbolism === Their names reflect their physical positioning in relation to their master during mountain treks: * '''Zenki''' ("Front Demon") walks ahead of En no Gyōja, clearing the path with an iron axe. He represents [[Yang]] (or ''Yō'' in Japanese), action, and protection. * '''Goki''' ("Back Demon") walks behind, carrying a water vessel (often containing Holy Water or ''aka'') and supplies. She represents [[Yin]] (or ''In'' in Japanese), receptivity, and sustenance.
Together, they represent the harmonious balance of dualities necessary for spiritual enlightenment within the Shugendō tradition.<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last=Earhart |first=H. Byron |title=A Religious Study of the Mount Haguro Sect of Shugendo |publisher=Sophia University |year=1970 |pages=25–30}}</ref>
== Iconography == In religious statuary and paintings (such as [[mandala]]s), the pair are almost always shown flanking En no Gyōja.
* ''Zenki'': Usually depicted with red skin, bulging eyes, and two horns. He wears a deer skin and carries a large iron [[axe]] meant to clear dense vegetation. * ''Goki'': Usually depicted with blue or green skin. In some depictions, she has no horns, appearing more human-like than her husband. She carries a water pitcher (''suibyo'') and sometimes a basket of fruits or firewood.
== Descendants == Unlike most ''oni'' in Japanese folklore, Zenki and Goki are traditionally believed to have established a human lineage. According to lore, after En no Gyōja passed away or ascended, the couple settled in the village of Shimokitayama in the Yoshino district of Nara Prefecture.
They are said to have had five children, who became the ancestors of the "Gokigen" (Five Demon relation) families. These families historically served as the protectors of the practice sites at [[Mount Ōmine]].<ref>{{cite book |last=Bouchy |first=Anne-Marie |title=Shugendô: The Way of the Mountain Monks |publisher=Sasshina |year=2000 |isbn=978-2954653526}}</ref>
Historically, residents of the Zenki settlement (Zenki-guchi) in Shimokitayama claim direct descent from the pair. This lineage was acknowledged during the [[Edo period]], granting the families specific privileges and tax exemptions from the Shogunate in exchange for their maintenance of the pilgrim lodges (''shukubo'').<ref name="Miyake">{{cite book |last=Miyake |first=Hitoshi |title=Shugendo: Essays on the Structure of Japanese Folk Religion |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, University of Michigan |year=2001 |isbn=978-0939512784 |pages=67–69}}</ref>
Because of this ancestry, these families traditionally do not use the phrase "Oni wa soto" (Demons out!) during the [[Setsubun]] bean-throwing festival; instead, they may say "Oni wa uchi" (Demons in) or simply remain silent, regarding the demons as honorable ancestors rather than monsters to be expelled.
== Popular culture == * ''Anime/Manga'': The characters appear in various supernatural manga, including ''[[Zenki]]'' (Kishin Dōji Zenki), where Zenki is the titular protagonist bound to a descendant of En no Gyōja. * ''Video Games'': They appear as demons or "Personas" in the ''[[Megami Tensei]]'' franchise. * ''Literature'': They are featured in the fantasy novel ''[[Teito Monogatari]]''.
== See also == * [[Shugendō]] * [[En no Gyōja]] * [[Oni (folklore)]] * [[Kishimojin]] (A Buddhist deity with a similar origin story involving a hidden child)
== References == {{Reflist}}
''Note: Generated using Google Gemini 3 Pro''.
== External links == * [https://www.narahaku.go.jp/english/collection/1183-0.html Nara National Museum - Statues of En no Gyoja, Zenki and Goki]

{{Infobox mythological creature | name = Zenki and Goki | image = En_no_Gyōja_sculpture_at_Kura-dera.jpg | image_size = 250px | caption = Statue of [[En no Gyōja]] flanked by Zenki (left) and Goki (right). | grouping = [[Oni (folklore)|Oni]] | mythological_origins = [[Japanese mythology]] | region = [[Japan]] | similar_creatures = [[Shikigami]] }}
'''Zenki''' {{nihongo|'''Zenki'''|前鬼||lit. "Front Demon"}} and {{nihongo|'''Goki'''|後鬼||lit. "Back Demon"}} are a husband and wife pair of [[Oni (folklore)|oni]] (demons or ogres) in [[Japanese folklore]] who serve as the familiars and protectors of the [[Shugendō]] founder [[En no Gyōja]].
They are frequently depicted in Japanese Buddhist art flanking En no Gyōja, often holding tools to assist him in his mountain asceticism. Zenki is traditionally depicted as male and red, while Goki is depicted as female and blue.<ref name="Miyake"/>
== Legend == According to tradition, Zenki and Goki were originally a pair of malevolent demons who lived in the Ikoma Mountains (or, in some variations, the [[Omine|Ōmine]] mountain range) near [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]]. They terrorized local villagers and stole their children.
[[En no Gyōja]], utilizing his spiritual powers, hid one of the demons' children (traditionally the youngest of five) in an iron cauldron to teach them the pain of losing a child. Overcome with grief, the demon couple begged for the child's return. En no Gyōja agreed on the condition that they reform their ways, cease killing, and follow the path of Buddha.<ref name="Blacker">{{cite book |last=Blacker |first=Carmen |title=The Catalpa Bow: Essays on Shamanistic Practices in Japan |publisher=Routledge |year=1999 |isbn=978-1873410851 |page=242}}</ref>
Following their conversion, they became En no Gyōja's devoted servants. Zenki, the husband, took the name '''Gikaku''' (義覚) or '''Giga''' (義学), and Goki, the wife, took the name '''Gigen''' (義賢) or '''Giken''' (義玄).<ref name="NaraMuseum">{{cite web |title=Statues of En no Gyoja, Zenki, and Goki |url=https://www.narahaku.go.jp/english/collection/1183-0.html |publisher=Nara National Museum |access-date=2023-10-27}}</ref>
=== Names and Symbolism === Their names reflect their physical positioning in relation to their master during mountain treks: * '''Zenki''' ("Front Demon") walks ahead of En no Gyōja, clearing the path with an iron axe. He represents [[Yang]] (or ''Yō'' in Japanese), action, and protection. * '''Goki''' ("Back Demon") walks behind, carrying a water vessel (often containing Holy Water or ''aka'') and supplies. She represents [[Yin]] (or ''In'' in Japanese), receptivity, and sustenance.
Together, they represent the harmonious balance of dualities necessary for spiritual enlightenment within the Shugendō tradition.<ref name="Earhart">{{cite book |last=Earhart |first=H. Byron |title=A Religious Study of the Mount Haguro Sect of Shugendo |publisher=Sophia University |year=1970 |pages=25–30}}</ref>
== Iconography == In religious statuary and paintings (such as [[mandala]]s), the pair are almost always shown flanking En no Gyōja.
* ''Zenki'': Usually depicted with red skin, bulging eyes, and two horns. He wears a deer skin and carries a large iron [[axe]] meant to clear dense vegetation. * ''Goki'': Usually depicted with blue or green skin. In some depictions, she has no horns, appearing more human-like than her husband. She carries a water pitcher (''suibyo'') and sometimes a basket of fruits or firewood.
== Descendants == Unlike most ''oni'' in Japanese folklore, Zenki and Goki are traditionally believed to have established a human lineage. According to lore, after En no Gyōja passed away or ascended, the couple settled in the village of Shimokitayama in the Yoshino district of Nara Prefecture.
They are said to have had five children, who became the ancestors of the "Gokigen" (Five Demon relation) families. These families historically served as the protectors of the practice sites at [[Mount Ōmine]].<ref>{{cite book |last=Bouchy |first=Anne-Marie |title=Shugendô: The Way of the Mountain Monks |publisher=Sasshina |year=2000 |isbn=978-2954653526}}</ref>
Historically, residents of the Zenki settlement (Zenki-guchi) in Shimokitayama claim direct descent from the pair. This lineage was acknowledged during the [[Edo period]], granting the families specific privileges and tax exemptions from the Shogunate in exchange for their maintenance of the pilgrim lodges (''shukubo'').<ref name="Miyake">{{cite book |last=Miyake |first=Hitoshi |title=Shugendo: Essays on the Structure of Japanese Folk Religion |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, University of Michigan |year=2001 |isbn=978-0939512784 |pages=67–69}}</ref>
Because of this ancestry, these families traditionally do not use the phrase "Oni wa soto" (Demons out!) during the [[Setsubun]] bean-throwing festival; instead, they may say "Oni wa uchi" (Demons in) or simply remain silent, regarding the demons as honorable ancestors rather than monsters to be expelled.
== Popular culture == * ''Anime/Manga'': The characters appear in various supernatural manga, including ''[[Zenki]]'' (Kishin Dōji Zenki), where Zenki is the titular protagonist bound to a descendant of En no Gyōja. * ''Video Games'': They appear as demons or "Personas" in the ''[[Megami Tensei]]'' franchise. * ''Literature'': They are featured in the fantasy novel ''[[Teito Monogatari]]''.
== See also == * [[Shugendō]] * [[En no Gyōja]] * [[Oni (folklore)]] * [[Kishimojin]] (A Buddhist deity with a similar origin story involving a hidden child)
